items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.q: What does inheritance allow a subclass to do?
items.correct: Reuse and extend the attributes and methods of its superclass.
items.random_wrong: Copy code from any unrelated class.
items.why: Students think inheritance overrides everything. It actually REUSES the parent's members (which can be added to or overridden), creating an 'is-a' relationship.

items.q: Why are passwords stored as hashes rather than plaintext?
items.correct: Hashes are one-way - even an attacker who steals them cannot easily recover passwords.
items.misconception: Hashes are encryption that can be decrypted with the right key.
items.random_wrong: Hashes compress the data to save space.
items.why: Students confuse hashing with encryption. Hashes have NO reverse function - you can verify a password by re-hashing, but cannot decrypt the stored hash to get the original.

items.q: Which is a high-level programming language?
items.why: Correct answer: Python. “Assembly” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Integrated Development Environment. “Internet Data Engine” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In Python, how many spaces are conventionally used for one level of indentation (PEP 8)?
items.why: The real figure is 4 spaces. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
